name: Whiskey
tagline: Grain, copper, and oak — the world's most collected family of spirits.
overview: Whiskey (spelled whisky in Scotland, Japan, and Canada) is a spirit distilled from a fermented mash of grain — barley, corn, rye, or wheat — and matured, almost always in oak casks. Every producing country writes its own legal definition, but the common thread is distillation below neutrality so that grain character survives, followed by time in wood, which contributes most of the color and much of the flavor. The result spans an enormous range: smoky Islay single malts, sweet corn-driven Kentucky bourbon, spicy rye, creamy triple-distilled Irish blends, and precise Japanese malts. Whiskey anchors classic cocktails such as the old fashioned and the Manhattan, dominates the collector and auction markets among spirits, and serves as the benchmark against which most other aged spirits are judged.
history: Distillation reached Ireland and Scotland with monastic and medical practice in the Middle Ages; the first surviving written record is a 1494 Scottish Exchequer Roll granting malt to Friar John Cor 'to make aqua vitae.' Centuries of taxation and smuggling followed, until the UK's 1823 Excise Act made licensed distilling practical. Aeneas Coffey's continuous still (1830) enabled cheap grain whisky and the blended Scotch that conquered world markets — helped when phylloxera devastated Cognac in the 1870s. American distillers built bourbon and rye traditions from the late 1700s; Japan opened its first malt distillery in 1923. After a late-20th-century slump, whiskey has been in global boom since the 2000s.
production: Grains are milled and mashed with hot water to convert starch to sugar (barley is malted first; other grains are usually cooked). Yeast ferments the resulting wort or mash into a beer of roughly 6–10% alcohol. Distillation follows in copper pot stills, run in batches for a richer spirit, or continuous column stills for a lighter one; the distiller keeps only the heart of the run. The new-make spirit then matures in oak — new charred barrels for bourbon, mostly ex-bourbon and ex-sherry casks elsewhere — where oxidation, extraction, and evaporation (the 'angel's share') do their slow work. Finally casks are married or blended, proofed with water, and bottled, typically at 40–46% ABV or at cask strength.

styles.name: Single malt
styles.description: Made from 100% malted barley at a single distillery, usually in pot stills; the template comes from Scotland but is now made worldwide.

styles.name: Blended whisky
styles.description: A marriage of richer malt or straight whiskies with lighter grain whisky; blends built the global Scotch, Irish, Canadian, and Japanese industries.

styles.name: Bourbon
styles.description: American whiskey from at least 51% corn aged in new charred oak — sweet, rounded, vanilla- and caramel-forward.

styles.name: Rye
styles.description: Spicier, drier whiskey led by rye grain; the historic backbone of American cocktail culture.

styles.name: Single pot still
styles.description: Ireland's signature: a mixed mash of malted and unmalted barley distilled in pot stills, giving a creamy, peppery character.

styles.name: Grain whisky
styles.description: Column-distilled whisky from corn or wheat, light in body; mostly used in blends but occasionally bottled on its own.

funFacts: The 1494 Scottish tax record covers enough malt to make around 1,500 bottles — whisky was already being produced at scale.
The spelling split is largely national habit: Ireland and the United States generally add the 'e', while Scotland, Japan, and Canada omit it — with exceptions like Maker's Mark.
Scotland loses roughly 2% of its maturing whisky to evaporation each year; in tropical climates the angel's share can exceed 10% annually.

name: Rum
tagline: Sugarcane's spirit — from funky Jamaican pot stills to crisp Spanish-style blends.
overview: Rum is distilled from sugarcane — usually molasses, the syrup left over from sugar refining, or fresh-pressed cane juice in the French Caribbean's rhum agricole. Because there is no single global standard, rum shows the widest stylistic spread of any major spirit: unaged whites built for daiquiris, long-aged tropical sippers, high-ester Jamaican pot-still rums with wild fruit-and-funk intensity, dark Demerara rums from wooden stills, and lighter Spanish-style rons. Labeling conventions differ by country — an age statement may mean the youngest component, an average, or a solera — and sweetening after distillation is permitted in some traditions and shunned in others. The Caribbean remains rum's heartland, but it is produced across Latin America, the Indian Ocean, Asia, and beyond.
history: Rum emerged in the 17th-century Caribbean, with Barbados usually credited; a 1651 account calls it 'kill-devil' or 'rumbullion.' Its early economics were inseparable from the transatlantic slave trade and plantation sugar, a legacy modern producers increasingly acknowledge. The Royal Navy issued a daily rum ration from the 17th century until 31 July 1970 — 'Black Tot Day.' In the French islands, rhum agricole from fresh cane juice grew after sugar-beet competition collapsed cane sugar prices in the 19th century. The tiki craze of the 1930s–60s made mixing rums famous, and since the 2000s single-estate bottlings, unsweetened aged expressions, and cask-strength releases have driven a premium revival.
production: Molasses (or fresh cane juice, which must be fermented quickly before it spoils) is diluted and fermented. Short, clean fermentations with cultured yeast give light rums; long, wild fermentations — sometimes fed with dunder and muck from previous runs, as in Jamaica — build the esters behind 'funk.' Distillation ranges from heavy double-retort pot stills to multi-column plants producing near-neutral spirit; Guyana's Demerara Distillers still runs heritage wooden stills. Aging usually happens in ex-bourbon casks, where tropical heat extracts flavor fast and evaporation can run 6% or more per year. Blending defines most house styles, and some producers add sugar or caramel — disclosure rules vary widely by country.

styles.name: Jamaican high-ester
styles.description: Long wild fermentations and pot stills yield intensely fruity, funky rums (the character locals call 'hogo'); Hampden and Worthy Park are benchmarks.

styles.name: Rhum agricole
styles.description: Distilled from fresh cane juice rather than molasses — grassy and vegetal; Martinique's version has held a French AOC since 1996.

styles.name: Demerara
styles.description: Guyanese rums, some from surviving wooden pot and column stills, known for dark, smoky-caramel depth.

styles.name: Spanish-style ron
styles.description: Lighter column-distilled rums of Cuba, Puerto Rico, the Dominican Republic, and Latin America; smooth, often solera-aged.

styles.name: Navy and overproof
styles.description: High-strength rums (57% and up) descended from naval blends and Caribbean overproof whites like Wray & Nephew.

styles.name: White mixing rum
styles.description: Unaged or charcoal-filtered rum built for daiquiris and mojitos; clean but not necessarily neutral.

funFacts: 'Grog' takes its name from Admiral Edward Vernon — nicknamed 'Old Grogram' for his cloak — who ordered the navy's rum watered down in 1740.
Martinique's rhum agricole AOC (1996) made it one of the first spirits outside mainland France governed by appellation law.
The Royal Navy's last daily rum ration was poured on 31 July 1970, still commemorated as Black Tot Day.

name: Gin
tagline: A juniper-led botanical canvas — from Dutch genever to the modern craft boom.
overview: Gin is a spirit whose predominant flavor must, by law in both the EU and US, come from juniper berries, layered with supporting botanicals such as coriander seed, angelica root, citrus peel, and orris. It is usually built on a neutral grain spirit and bottled at a minimum of 37.5% ABV in the EU (40% in the US). 'London Dry' describes a method, not a place: all flavor must come from distillation with botanicals, with essentially nothing but water added afterward. Gin's range runs from classic juniper-forward styles through soft Plymouth and sweetened Old Tom to malty Dutch genever and cucumber-and-rose contemporary gins. It is arguably the most cocktail-central of all spirits — the martini, negroni, and gin and tonic are unthinkable without it.
history: Juniper spirits began as medicine; the Dutch turned them into genever in the 16th and 17th centuries, and English soldiers fighting alongside the Dutch brought home a taste for 'Dutch courage.' After William of Orange took the English throne in 1688, cheap domestic distilling exploded into the Gin Craze of the early 1700s — London's per-capita consumption became a public health crisis, satirized in Hogarth's 'Gin Lane' (1751) and curbed by successive Gin Acts. Column stills and better neutral spirit produced the drier London style in the 19th century, while gin and tonic spread with quinine-drinking British India. A craft renaissance beginning around 1999–2009 (Hendrick's, Sipsmith) multiplied distilleries worldwide.
production: Producers start with neutral spirit — usually grain — and add botanicals by steeping them in the still (maceration), suspending them in baskets so vapor passes through (vapor infusion, as in Carter-head stills), or both. Each botanical can be distilled separately and blended, or everything can go in one run; 'one-shot' gins distill at final recipe strength while 'multi-shot' concentrates are diluted with more neutral spirit. Juniper must remain the dominant note for the product to be gin at all. Distilled gin is then cut to bottling strength; London Dry permits no post-distillation flavoring and only trace sweetening. Compounded 'bathtub' gins skip redistillation and simply infuse or mix essences into spirit.

styles.name: London Dry
styles.description: The benchmark: juniper-forward, bone dry, all flavor from distillation. Made anywhere in the world despite the name.

styles.name: Plymouth
styles.description: A softer, earthier, slightly sweeter style historically tied to the Plymouth distillery in Devon, once protected by geographical indication.

styles.name: Old Tom
styles.description: The lightly sweetened 19th-century style that bridges genever and London Dry; essential in a proper Tom Collins or Martinez.

styles.name: Genever
styles.description: The Dutch and Belgian ancestor, built on malt wine — richer and grainier, sold as oude (old-style, maltier) or jonge (lighter).

styles.name: Navy strength
styles.description: Bottled at about 57% ABV, the strength at which gunpowder soaked in spirit will still ignite — hence the naval 'proof.'

styles.name: Contemporary / New Western
styles.description: Modern gins that keep juniper legal-minimum prominent while spotlighting other botanicals — cucumber, rose, tea, or foraged local plants.

funFacts: Hogarth's 1751 'Gin Lane' engraving was paired with a companion piece, 'Beer Street,' portraying beer as the wholesome alternative.
Navy strength (57% ABV) comes from the old gunpowder test: spirit at that strength won't stop soaked gunpowder from firing.
The Philippines is the world's largest gin market by volume, driven by Ginebra San Miguel.

name: Vodka
tagline: The pursuit of purity — a clean spirit of grain, potato, or grape built for mixing.
overview: Vodka is a clear spirit distilled to near neutrality and diluted with water, bottled at a minimum of 37.5% ABV in the EU and 40% in the US. It can be made from any agricultural raw material — wheat, rye, corn, and potatoes are traditional, but grapes, sugar beets, and even whey appear. Because distillation runs to 95–96% alcohol, differences between vodkas are subtle, expressed more in texture and finish than in overt flavor: wheat tends creamy, rye peppery, potato earthy and full. That neutrality made vodka the dominant cocktail base of the late 20th century, from the Moscow mule to the espresso martini, and the vehicle for endless flavored variants. In its Polish, Russian, and Nordic homelands it is traditionally drunk neat, chilled, with food.
history: Poland and Russia both claim vodka's origin, and the argument is centuries old; the Polish word 'wódka' appears in court documents from 1405, with early distillates serving as medicines. Vodka matured into the everyday spirit of the 'vodka belt' from Scandinavia through Russia. The West's conversion came later: the Smirnov family's brand, re-established in exile after the Russian Revolution, reached America in the 1930s, and the Moscow mule's 1940s marketing push plus vodka's mixability drove it past whiskey as the top-selling US spirit by 1976. Absolut's art-driven advertising (from 1979) and Grey Goose (1997) invented the premium and super-premium tiers that still define the category.
production: The base — grain, potato, or another sugar or starch source — is fermented, then distilled in continuous column stills to at least 95–96% ABV, at which point little but ethanol and water remains. The spirit is cut to bottling strength with purified water, and most producers filter it, classically through activated charcoal; birch charcoal, quartz sand, and even lava rock feature in various traditions. Some craft distillers deliberately distill fewer times or skip heavy filtration to keep raw-material character. 'Number of distillations' marketing usually counts column passes or plates rather than separate runs. Flavored vodkas are made by maceration or by adding natural extracts after distillation.

styles.name: Wheat vodka
styles.description: The classic Russian and French base — soft, faintly sweet, with a creamy texture (Grey Goose uses Picardy wheat).

styles.name: Rye vodka
styles.description: Poland's traditional grain, giving a drier, subtly spicy, nutty profile.

styles.name: Potato vodka
styles.description: Fuller-bodied and earthy with an oily mouthfeel; historically associated with Poland.

styles.name: Corn vodka
styles.description: Slightly sweet and round; Tito's built the modern American craft-vodka boom on corn spirit.

styles.name: Flavored vodka
styles.description: From centuries-old Polish żubrówka (bison grass) and Russian pepper vodkas to the citrus and berry wave of the 1990s–2000s.

styles.name: Aquavit
styles.description: Vodka's Scandinavian cousin: the same neutral base, but flavored with caraway or dill by law — Iceland's Brennivín is a stark example.

funFacts: The word vodka is a diminutive of the Slavic 'voda' — literally 'little water.'

The story that Dmitri Mendeleev scientifically set vodka at 40% is a myth; the standard came from 19th-century Russian tax convenience, and his dissertation was on ethanol-water chemistry, not recipes.
Vodka can legally be made from almost anything agricultural — including grapes, milk whey, and honey.

name: Agave Spirits
tagline: Mexico's slow-grown spirits — roasted hearts of a plant that takes a decade to ripen.
overview: Agave spirits are distilled from the cooked, fermented hearts (piñas) of agave plants — succulents that need roughly six to thirty years to mature and die after a single harvest, making this the slowest agriculture in the spirits world. The family is led by tequila (blue agave only, under a 1974 denomination of origin) and mezcal (dozens of agave species, famously pit-roasted and smoky), alongside regional cousins raicilla from Jalisco's coast and bacanora from Sonora; sotol, made from the botanically distinct desert spoon plant, is traditionally grouped with them. Flavors run from cooked sweet-potato sweetness and bright pepper to deep earth, minerality, and smoke. Agave spirits have been the fastest-premiumizing category of the 21st century, driven by cocktails, sipping culture, and interest in traditional production.
history: Mesoamerican cultures fermented agave sap into pulque millennia before European contact, treating the plant and its drink as sacred. Distillation of cooked agave began in the colonial era — scholars debate whether Filipino stills arriving via Manila galleons or Spanish alembics came first, and some argue for limited pre-Hispanic distillation. The town of Tequila industrialized its 'vino de mezcal,' with the Cuervo family licensed by the Spanish crown in 1795. Mexico protected tequila with a denomination of origin in 1974 and mezcal in 1994. The margarita made tequila a US staple after the mid-20th century, and the 2000s–2020s brought single-village mezcal exports, additive-free debates, and a celebrity-brand gold rush.
production: Jimadores harvest mature agaves with a long-bladed coa, shearing the leaves to leave the piña. Cooking converts inulin to fermentable sugars: tequila uses brick ovens or steel autoclaves, mezcal roasts piñas for days in wood-fired earthen pits, and industrial plants may use diffusers that extract sugars from raw fiber. The cooked agave is crushed — by roller mill, by a stone tahona wheel, or by hand mallets — and fermented with cultivated or wild yeasts, sometimes fiber and all. Two distillations are standard, in copper pots, stainless columns, or small clay stills for ancestral mezcal. Most mezcal is bottled young; tequila's reposado, añejo, and extra añejo classes rest in oak.

styles.name: Tequila
styles.description: Blue Weber agave spirit from Jalisco and four neighboring states, from crisp blancos to long-aged extra añejos.

styles.name: Mezcal
styles.description: The broader traditional family, centered on Oaxaca — pit-roasted, often smoky, made from espadín and wild agaves.

styles.name: Raicilla
styles.description: Western Jalisco's village spirit, historically sold as 'root stock' to dodge taxes; earned its own DO in 2019.

styles.name: Bacanora
styles.description: Sonoran mezcal-style spirit from Agave angustifolia, illegal for much of the 20th century and protected since 2000.

styles.name: Sotol
styles.description: Chihuahua and Coahuila's spirit from the desert spoon (Dasylirion) — not a true agave, but part of the same tradition.

styles.name: Pulque
styles.description: The undistilled ancestor: fresh agave sap fermented into a lightly alcoholic, viscous drink still sold in pulquerías.

funFacts: Agaves are monocarpic — they flower once and die — so spirits agave is harvested just before the plant sends up its towering flower stalk.
Nectar-feeding bats are key agave pollinators; 'bat-friendly' projects encourage growers to let some plants flower to preserve genetic diversity.
Because blue agave is propagated from clones, tequila's raw material is a near-monoculture — a long-term disease risk the industry actively debates.

name: Brandy
tagline: Distilled wine and orchard fruit — the original luxury spirit.
overview: Brandy is spirit distilled from fermented fruit — classically grape wine, as in Cognac, Armagnac, Spanish Brandy de Jerez, and American brandy, but the family also includes pomace brandies like grappa and marc (distilled from pressed grape skins), apple and pear brandies like Calvados, unaged grape pisco, and the clear fruit eaux-de-vie of Central Europe. The name comes from the Dutch 'brandewijn,' burnt wine. Most fine brandy is aged in oak, picking up amber color and flavors of dried fruit, spice, and rancio with decades in cask, though eaux-de-vie and pisco are bottled clear. Brandy was the world's prestige spirit before whisky's rise and remains the base of classic cocktails such as the sidecar and brandy crusta.
history: Distillation of wine spread through medieval Europe via alchemical and medical texts, and by the 16th century Dutch traders were concentrating wine into brandewijn so it could survive sea voyages — the trade that built Cognac and Armagnac. Fruit growers from Normandy to the Balkans applied the same stills to apples, pears, and plums. The phylloxera louse devastated French vineyards from the 1870s, collapsing brandy supply and handing world markets to Scotch whisky and gin. Spanish houses matured brandy in sherry soleras at Jerez; Peru and Chile developed (and still dispute) pisco. Today Cognac dominates the luxury tier while craft distillers revive regional fruit-brandy traditions.
production: Fruit is fermented dry — fine brandy regions forbid added sugar — and the base wine or fruit wine is distilled either twice in copper pot stills (Cognac's method) or once in small column stills (Armagnac's), with pomace brandies distilled from grape skins and eaux-de-vie from whole-fruit mashes. Distillation strength matters: lower proof keeps more fruit congeners. Aging in oak — new casks first, then older wood — builds color and spice, with humid or dry cellars steering the balance of strength and softness. Blending across ages and vineyards is the norm, with age statements keyed to the youngest component. Clear fruit brandies skip wood entirely to preserve orchard aromatics.

styles.name: Cognac
styles.description: Double pot-distilled ugni blanc wine from the Charente, aged in French oak; the world's reference luxury brandy.

styles.name: Armagnac
styles.description: Gascony's older, earthier cousin — usually single-distilled in a small column still and often sold as single vintages.

styles.name: Calvados
styles.description: Norman apple (and pear) brandy distilled from cider and aged in oak.

styles.name: Brandy de Jerez
styles.description: Spanish brandy matured in ex-sherry casks through a solera system — dark, raisiny, and generous.

styles.name: Pomace brandy
styles.description: Grappa (Italy) and marc (France), distilled from the skins and seeds left after winemaking; fiery when young, graceful when aged.

styles.name: Eau-de-vie and pisco
styles.description: Clear, unaged fruit and grape brandies — kirsch, poire Williams, slivovitz, and South America's pisco — prized for pure fruit aromatics.

funFacts: The word brandy comes from the Dutch 'brandewijn' — 'burnt (distilled) wine' — coined by the traders who shipped it.
Peru and Chile have argued for decades over which country is pisco's rightful home; both regulate the name domestically.
Some poire Williams bottles contain a whole pear, grown inside a bottle tied onto the tree over the orchard season.

name: Liqueurs
tagline: Sweetened, flavored spirits — the flavor pantry of the cocktail world.
overview: Liqueurs are spirits that have been sweetened and flavored — with herbs, fruit, citrus peel, flowers, coffee, nuts, spices, or cream. EU law requires at least 100 grams of sugar per liter; alcohol ranges widely, from gentle 15% cream liqueurs to Green Chartreuse at 55%. Few are drunk in quantity on their own: their role is as modifiers and accents — the orange liqueur in a margarita or sidecar, the maraschino in a last word, the amaro after dinner. The category spans industrial triple secs, single-flavor fruit liqueurs, Italy's vast bitter amaro tradition, and centuries-old monastic recipes whose full formulas remain secret. For bartenders, liqueurs are the spice rack that makes classic cocktails possible.
history: Liqueurs descend from medieval monastic and apothecary elixirs, where alcohol preserved medicinal herbs — pleasure arrived later. The Carthusians received their famous manuscript in 1605 and were selling an Elixir Végétal by 1737; Bénédictine, despite its abbey legend, was created commercially by Alexandre Le Grand in 1863. Italy built the amaro tradition through the 19th century — Fernet-Branca (1845), Campari (around 1860), Aperol (1919) — while Curaçao's bitter oranges, via Dutch trade, begat the orange liqueur family. The golden age of cocktails made liqueurs indispensable, and the modern craft revival added new stars like St-Germain elderflower (2007), sometimes called 'bartender's ketchup' for its ubiquity.
production: A base spirit — neutral alcohol, brandy, whiskey, or rum — is flavored by maceration (steeping botanicals or fruit), percolation, or distillation of aromatics, and often by a combination. The flavored spirit is then sweetened with sugar, honey, or syrup, sometimes colored, and rested so components marry. Herbal liqueurs may combine dozens of botanicals in guarded proportions; Chartreuse reportedly uses 130. Cream liqueurs like Baileys (launched 1974) emulsify dairy cream with spirit for shelf-stable richness. Quality turns on real versus artificial flavoring, the freshness of fruit and peel, and the balance of sugar against alcohol and bitterness.

styles.name: Herbal and monastic
styles.description: Complex multi-botanical recipes like Chartreuse and Bénédictine, born as elixirs and still made to secret formulas.

styles.name: Amaro
styles.description: Italy's bittersweet family — gentian, wormwood, citrus, and roots — from aperitivo-light Aperol to bracing Fernet.

styles.name: Orange liqueur
styles.description: Triple sec, curaçao, and brandy-based Grand Marnier; the most-called liqueur family in classic cocktails.

styles.name: Coffee and cream
styles.description: Kahlúa, Mr Black, and Baileys — dessert-leaning liqueurs behind the espresso martini and Irish cream serves.

styles.name: Anise
styles.description: Sambuca, pastis, and ouzo's sweetened kin, turning cloudy with water as anethole comes out of solution.

styles.name: Fruit and floral
styles.description: Cassis, maraschino, limoncello, and elderflower — single-note liqueurs that carry regional fruit traditions.

funFacts: Only two Carthusian monks at a time are said to know the complete Chartreuse formula.
The color chartreuse is named after the liqueur, not the other way around.
Amaro simply means 'bitter' in Italian — the category ranges from about 11% to over 40% ABV.

name: Wine
tagline: Fermented grapes, infinite expression — humanity's oldest fine beverage.
overview: Wine is the fermented juice of grapes, overwhelmingly the Eurasian species Vitis vinifera, made in styles from bone-dry reds and whites through rosé, sparkling, sweet, and fortified. Alcohol typically runs 11–15% for still wines and up to about 20% fortified. No other drink carries wine's cultural weight: it is agriculture bottled, and the concept of terroir — that soil, site, and season are legible in the glass — underpins appellation systems like France's AOC, Italy's DOC/DOCG, and America's AVAs, which regulate where and how wines are made. Vintage variation, the age-worthiness of great bottles, and a global spectrum from mass-market brands to single-vineyard rarities make wine both an everyday staple and the most collected beverage on earth.
history: The earliest chemical evidence of grape wine comes from Georgia around 6000 BC, with clay qvevri vessels still used there today; a complete winery some 6,100 years old was excavated at Armenia's Areni-1 cave. Phoenicians, Greeks, and Romans spread viticulture across the Mediterranean, and medieval monasteries — especially in Burgundy — mapped vineyards with a precision that still defines them. Bordeaux's 1855 classification ranked châteaux for a world's fair and stuck. Phylloxera, an American root louse, nearly destroyed European viticulture from the 1860s until grafting onto resistant American rootstock saved it. The 1976 Judgment of Paris tasting legitimized the New World, and winemaking is now genuinely global.
production: Grapes are harvested by hand or machine at a chosen balance of sugar and acid, then crushed and pressed — before fermentation for whites, after skin maceration for reds, with rosé in between. Yeast, wild or cultured, ferments sugars to alcohol in stainless steel, oak, concrete, or clay; most reds also undergo malolactic conversion, softening acidity. Élevage follows — months on lees or in barrels — before fining, filtration, and bottling. Sparkling wines gain bubbles from a second fermentation; fortified wines have grape spirit added; sweet wines rely on noble rot, drying, freezing, or stopped fermentation. The winemaker's thousand small choices, on top of the vineyard's raw material, define style.

styles.name: Red
styles.description: Fermented on dark skins for color and tannin — from feather-light gamay to dense, cellar-worthy cabernet.

styles.name: White
styles.description: Pressed clean and fermented cool; spans racy riesling, mineral chablis, and rich barrel-aged chardonnay.

styles.name: Rosé
styles.description: Brief skin contact gives pink color and red-fruit lift with white-wine freshness.

styles.name: Sparkling
styles.description: Champagne, cava, prosecco, and pét-nat — wines carbonated by a second (or unfinished first) fermentation.

styles.name: Fortified
styles.description: Port, sherry, madeira, and vermouth — wines strengthened with spirit, often oxidative and long-lived.

styles.name: Dessert and skin-contact
styles.description: Botrytized sauternes and tokaji, icewine, and amber 'orange' wines fermented on white-grape skins.

funFacts: Nearly every European vine today grows grafted onto American rootstock — the fix that ended the 19th-century phylloxera catastrophe.
Georgia's 8,000-year-old qvevri winemaking method is inscribed on UNESCO's Intangible Cultural Heritage list.
A standard bottle of wine represents roughly 600 grapes — about one vine's worth in strictly managed vineyards.

name: Beer
tagline: Water, malt, hops, and yeast — the world's most popular alcoholic drink.
overview: Beer is brewed from malted grain — chiefly barley, with wheat, rice, corn, and oats in supporting roles — flavored and preserved with hops, and fermented with yeast. It is the most consumed alcoholic beverage on earth and, by many accounts, the third most popular drink overall after water and tea. Most beer sits between 4% and 6% ABV, but the family runs from 0.5% table beers to imperial stouts and Belgian quadrupels above 12%. The great divide is yeast: ales ferment warm and fast with top-fermenting strains, lagers cold and slow with bottom-fermenting ones. Styles span crisp pilsners, bitter IPAs, roasty stouts, spicy Belgian abbey ales, tart lambics, and everything the modern craft movement keeps inventing.
history: Beer is as old as agriculture: Mesopotamians sang the Hymn to Ninkasi — brewing goddess and recipe in one — nearly 4,000 years ago, and Egyptian pyramid workers drew beer rations. Medieval European brewing moved from herb mixtures (gruit) to hops, and Bavaria's 1516 Reinheitsgebot limited ingredients to barley, hops, and water (yeast being then unknown). Pilsen's golden lager of 1842 became the world's template. Industrial consolidation homogenized 20th-century beer until American homebrewing was legalized in 1978, seeding a craft revolution — Sierra Nevada's 1980 pale ale, the IPA boom, and thousands of new breweries worldwide, alongside unbroken traditions like Belgium's Trappist abbeys.
production: Barley is malted — steeped, germinated, and kilned — to develop enzymes and flavor, from pale malt to black roast. The brewer mashes milled malt with hot water, converting starch to sugar, then drains off sweet wort and boils it with hops: early additions give bitterness, late ones aroma. The cooled wort is fermented — ale yeasts around 15–24°C for days, lager yeasts at 7–13°C followed by weeks of cold conditioning. Beer is then carbonated, filtered or left hazy, and packaged; some styles referment in the bottle (Trappist ales), pour on nitrogen (Guinness), or ferment spontaneously with wild yeast in open coolships (lambic).

styles.name: Lager and pilsner
styles.description: Cold-fermented, clean, and crisp — from Czech pilsner and German helles to the pale lagers that dominate world volume.

styles.name: Pale ale
styles.description: Balanced, hop-accented ales; the American version, built on citrusy Cascade hops, launched craft brewing.

styles.name: IPA
styles.description: India pale ale, the craft era's flagship — assertively hopped, from resinous West Coast to soft, juicy New England styles.

styles.name: Stout and porter
styles.description: Dark ales built on roasted malt — dry Irish stout, chocolatey porter, and barrel-aged imperial versions.

styles.name: Belgian and Trappist
styles.description: Expressive, yeast-driven ales — dubbel, tripel, quadrupel — a handful still brewed inside monastery walls.

styles.name: Sour and wild
styles.description: Lambic, gueuze, Flanders reds, and modern kettle sours — beers shaped by wild yeast and bacteria.

funFacts: Bavaria's 1516 Reinheitsgebot is often called the world's oldest food-purity regulation still invoked today.
Weihenstephan in Bavaria claims brewing since 1040, making it the world's oldest continuously operating brewery.
Only about ten breweries worldwide may use the 'Authentic Trappist Product' logo, and the number shifts as monasteries open and close.

name: Sake
tagline: Japan's rice brew — koji mold, soft water, and polishing-ratio precision.
overview: Sake (nihonshu in Japan) is brewed — not distilled — from rice, water, yeast, and koji, rice inoculated with the mold Aspergillus oryzae that converts starch to sugar. Uniquely, saccharification and fermentation happen simultaneously in one tank ('multiple parallel fermentation'), naturally reaching around 17–20% alcohol, the highest of any traditional fermented drink, before most sake is diluted to 15–16% for bottling. Quality grades track the polishing ratio — how much of each rice grain is milled away — and whether a small amount of distiller's alcohol is added: junmai means pure rice; ginjo (rice polished to 60% or less remaining) and daiginjo (50% or less) denote the fragrant premium tiers. Styles run from rich and savory to strikingly fruity, served anywhere from lightly warmed to well chilled.
history: Rice brewing in Japan goes back roughly two millennia, refined at imperial courts and temples through the Nara and Heian periods. Edo-era brewers systematized cold-season brewing (kanzukuri) and practiced pasteurization (hi-ire) centuries before Pasteur explained it. Meiji-era science brought pure yeast strains and modern milling, and the 20th century saw mass-market sake with added alcohol during rice shortages, followed by the premium ginjo revolution of the 1980s as vertical milling machines made high polish practical. Domestic consumption has declined for decades, but exports and international prestige keep rising, and in December 2024 UNESCO added traditional sake brewing knowledge to its Intangible Cultural Heritage list.
production: Brown rice is polished to the target seimaibuai (the percentage remaining), washed, steeped with stopwatch timing, and steamed. About a fifth becomes koji, cultivated for roughly two days in a warm, humid koji room. Brewers then build a yeast starter (shubo) — quick modern sokujō with added lactic acid, or the slower traditional kimoto and yamahai methods that culture wild lactic bacteria. The main mash (moromi) is built in three staged additions over four days and ferments cold for two to five weeks. The finished mash is pressed, settled, usually charcoal-filtered, pasteurized (or left as fresh nama), rested, diluted, and bottled.

styles.name: Junmai
styles.description: Pure rice sake with no added alcohol — typically fuller, more savory, and food-friendly.

styles.name: Honjozo
styles.description: Rice polished to 70% or less with a small addition of distiller's alcohol, giving a lighter, drier profile that warms well.

styles.name: Ginjo / Junmai Ginjo
styles.description: Polished to 60% or less and fermented cold and slow for melon-and-apple aromatics.

styles.name: Daiginjo / Junmai Daiginjo
styles.description: The apex grade, polished to 50% or less — aromatic, delicate, and precise; usually served chilled.

styles.name: Nigori
styles.description: Coarsely filtered so rice solids remain — cloudy, creamy, often gently sweet.

styles.name: Namazake
styles.description: Unpasteurized sake with vivid, fresh flavors; requires refrigeration and is often released seasonally.

funFacts: Dassai 23's rice is polished until only 23% of each grain remains — more than three-quarters milled away before brewing begins.
Breweries hang a cedar ball (sugidama) at the door; it browns over the season, tracking the new sake's maturity.
Sake ferments to around 20% alcohol naturally — stronger than any wine — thanks to koji feeding yeast sugar continuously.

name: Soju
tagline: Korea's everyday spirit — the best-selling liquor category on Earth.
overview: Soju is Korea's clear national spirit, traditionally pot-distilled from rice with a nuruk fermentation starter. The mainstream modern version is 'diluted' soju: high-purity neutral spirit — often from sweet potatoes or tapioca — cut with water to roughly 16–20% ABV and rounded with a trace of sweetener, sold in the iconic green 360 ml bottle and drunk in shared shots alongside food. Jinro's Chamisul is the world's best-selling spirit brand by volume, moving around 100 million nine-liter cases a year. Alongside the cheap-and-social mainstream sit heritage distilled sojus like Andong soju and a premium rice-soju revival (Hwayo, Tokki), plus the fruit-flavored wave that took off in the mid-2010s.
history: Distillation reached Korea in the 13th century with the Mongol invasions, via the arak tradition; Andong, a Mongol logistics base, remains soju's heritage capital. For centuries soju was a rice spirit made at home and by regional distillers. The pivotal modern rupture came in 1965, when the Grain Management Law banned distilling rice to protect food supplies — producers switched to diluting imported neutral spirit, creating today's green-bottle style; the ban ended in the 1990s, but the diluted format had won the market. Soju's strength has drifted downward from 25% toward the teens, flavored sojus boomed after 2015, and the Korean wave has carried exports worldwide.
production: Traditional soju starts with nuruk, a pressed cake of wheat or rice hosting wild molds, yeasts, and bacteria, which ferments a rice mash; the brew is then distilled once in a sot fitted with a soju-gori clay still head, or in modern pot or vacuum stills that preserve delicate aromas, then rested and bottled at 20–45%. Diluted soju instead begins with continuous-still ethanol of about 95%, which is filtered (often through charcoal), blended with purified water to 16–20%, and finished with tiny amounts of sweeteners such as erythritol or aspartame and flavor compounds for smoothness. Fruit sojus add flavorings and sugar at lower strength.

styles.name: Diluted soju (huiseok)
styles.description: The green-bottle standard: neutral spirit, water, and a touch of sweetener at 16–20% ABV.

styles.name: Traditional distilled soju
styles.description: Single-distilled rice soju made with nuruk — fuller, aromatic, and closer to the pre-1965 heritage.

styles.name: Andong soju
styles.description: The famous regional style from Andong, historically stronger (around 45%) and designated an intangible cultural asset.

styles.name: Flavored soju
styles.description: Grapefruit, green grape, peach, and yogurt variants around 12–14%, a category that exploded after 2015.

styles.name: Premium rice soju
styles.description: Modern craft bottlings like Hwayo, vacuum- or pot-distilled from Korean rice, aimed at sipping and cocktails.

funFacts: Jinro regularly tops global rankings as the world's best-selling spirit brand — outselling the biggest vodka and whisky brands combined counts of cases.
Korean soju makers share a standardized green bottle so empties can be washed and reused across brands.
Soju etiquette is codified: pour with two hands, never fill your own glass, and turn away from elders when drinking.

name: Hard Cider
tagline: Fermented apples — orchard wine with a brewer's soul.
overview: Hard cider is fermented apple juice, typically 4.5–8.5% ABV (perry is its pear-based sibling). True cider apples — bittersweets and bittersharps too tannic to eat — give structure that dessert fruit can't, which is why the great traditions cluster where those orchards grow: England's tannic West Country ciders, Normandy and Brittany's gently sweet keeved cidre, Spain's sharp, wild sidra natural poured from arm's length, and North America's modern clean, fruit-forward styles alongside intense ice cider. Styles run bone-dry and still to champagne-method sparklers. The United Kingdom drinks more cider than any other country, while the US market rebuilt itself almost from zero after 2010.
history: Cider came to Britain with the Normans after 1066 and became the West Country's farmhouse staple, with workers partly paid in cider into the 19th century. Colonial America ran on it — orchards outnumbered breweries, and John Adams reportedly began each day with a draft; Johnny Appleseed's seedling apples were cider fruit, not eating fruit. American cider collapsed under German lager immigration, temperance, and Prohibition's orchard destruction, surviving in Europe's unbroken English, French, and Spanish traditions (Asturias and the Basque Country trace theirs to antiquity). Quebec invented modern ice cider in the 1990s, and craft producers revived American cider through the 2010s.
production: Apples are milled and pressed, classically blending sharp, sweet, and bittersweet varieties for balance. Fermentation runs cool and slow with wild or cultured yeast, often followed by malolactic softening. The French keeving technique deliberately starves the ferment by trapping pectin and nutrients in a gel, leaving natural sweetness at low alcohol. Ciders finish still, lightly pétillant, or fully sparkling — force-carbonated, tank-conditioned, or bottle-fermented like champagne. Spanish sidra natural skips carbonation entirely and gets its sparkle from the long aerating pour. Ice cider concentrates juice by winter freezing before fermentation; perry follows the same road with tannic perry pears.

styles.name: English West Country
styles.description: Tannic, full-bodied ciders from bittersweet apples — from polished bottlings to rustic farmhouse scrumpy.

styles.name: French cidre
styles.description: Normandy and Brittany's keeved ciders — naturally sweet, low-alcohol, and delicately sparkling; Pays d'Auge holds an AOC.

styles.name: Spanish sidra natural
styles.description: Asturian and Basque still cider — sharp, funky, and traditionally poured from a height to aerate it.

styles.name: Modern American
styles.description: Clean, sessionable ciders from dessert fruit, plus a craft wing reviving heirloom and foraged apples.

styles.name: Ice cider
styles.description: Quebec's contribution: juice concentrated by natural freezing, fermented into an intense, sweet-tart dessert cider.

styles.name: Perry
styles.description: The pear equivalent, made from gnarled perry pears; delicate, floral, and increasingly rare.

funFacts: Asturias' escanciar pour — bottle overhead, glass at the knee — exists to aerate still sidra into a brief fizz.
Johnny Appleseed's trees grew from seed and bore bitter fruit, useful mainly for cider — planting orchards also secured frontier land claims.
'Scrumpy' likely comes from 'scrump,' dialect for windfall or pilfered apples.

name: Ready-to-Drink
tagline: Cocktails in a can — seltzers, highballs, and premixed classics.
overview: Ready-to-drink (RTD) covers packaged, premixed alcoholic drinks: hard seltzers, canned and bottled cocktails, flavored malt beverages, wine-based spritzes, and Japan's chū-hai highballs. Bases vary — fermented malt or cane sugar keeps US taxes low, while spirit-based RTDs use real vodka, tequila, or whiskey — and ABV typically runs 4–12%. The category exploded after 2016 on hard seltzer's low-calorie, gluten-free pitch, then matured toward genuine spirit quality: vodka-soda brands like High Noon and bartender-grade bottled cocktails such as On The Rocks. Convenience, portion control, and honest labeling drive the appeal; the trade-off is that quality spans from sugary alcopops to drinks that genuinely rival a bar pour.
history: Premixed drinks are old — Heublein sold bottled 'club cocktails' as early as 1892 — but the modern arc starts with 1980s wine coolers, which vanished after a 1991 US excise tax hike pushed makers to malt bases. Japan canned the chū-hai (shōchū highball) in the early 1980s, building a huge domestic RTD culture. The 1990s brought alcopops like Smirnoff Ice; 2016 launched the hard seltzer era, and by the 'hard seltzer summer' of 2019 White Claw was rationing shipments. Spirit-based canned cocktails surged during the 2020 pandemic as bars closed, and major distillers, brewers, and even soda companies now compete across the category worldwide.
production: Producers choose a base first: fermented malt or sugar brew (taxed like beer in the US), wine, or distilled spirits (taxed higher, but cleaner-tasting). The base is filtered — sugar-brew and malt bases heavily, to near neutrality — then blended with flavors: fruit essences, juices, citrus oils, or full cocktail recipes with bitters and liqueurs. Water, acids, and sometimes sweeteners balance the blend, which is carbonated (still for some cocktails), pasteurized or sterile-filtered for stability, and canned or bottled. Formulation must survive months in aluminum — citrus oils, proteins, and can-liner chemistry all get stability-tested before release.

styles.name: Hard seltzer
styles.description: Carbonated water with alcohol from fermented cane sugar or malt and light fruit essence — typically ~5% ABV and around 100 calories.

styles.name: Spirit-based canned cocktail
styles.description: Real vodka, tequila, gin, or whiskey with mixers — vodka sodas, margaritas, and highballs led by brands like High Noon.

styles.name: Flavored malt beverage
styles.description: The alcopop lineage — Smirnoff Ice, Twisted Tea, Mike's Hard — built on filtered malt base for tax efficiency.

styles.name: Chū-hai
styles.description: Japan's shōchū-and-soda highballs in endless fruit flavors, canned since the early 1980s and a konbini staple.

styles.name: Bottled premixed classics
styles.description: Full-strength cocktails ready to pour — old fashioneds, negronis, and manhattans, sometimes barrel-aged.

funFacts: White Claw's 2019 boom outran its supply chain, forcing publicly acknowledged nationwide shortages.
Chū-hai is short for 'shōchū highball,' though many modern versions use vodka instead.
US federal excise tax differs sharply by base alcohol, which is why so many RTDs ferment sugar or malt instead of using spirits.

name: Bourbon
tagline: America's native spirit — corn, new charred oak, and Kentucky limestone water.
overview: Bourbon is American whiskey defined by federal standards of identity: a mash of at least 51% corn, distilled at no more than 160 proof (80% ABV), entered into charred new oak containers at no more than 125 proof, and bottled at 80 proof or above, with nothing added but water. It can be made anywhere in the United States, though about 95% comes from Kentucky, where limestone-filtered water and swinging seasonal temperatures shaped the tradition. 'Straight' bourbon adds a two-year minimum age and bars all additives. The new-char oak requirement gives bourbon its signature caramel, vanilla, and baking-spice sweetness over corn richness. Congress declared bourbon 'a distinctive product of the United States' in 1964, and it anchors the old fashioned, the mint julep, and the modern whiskey collecting boom.
history: Corn whiskey followed Scots-Irish and German settlers into Kentucky in the late 1700s; how it became 'bourbon' — via Bourbon County shipping ports or New Orleans' Bourbon Street — remains genuinely debated. Charred-barrel aging was established by the early 1800s, and Dr. James Crow systematized sour-mash methods in the 1830s. The Bottled-in-Bond Act of 1897, an early consumer-protection law, guaranteed unadulterated whiskey; Prohibition reduced the industry to six medicinal licensees. After mid-century dominance and a 1970s–80s slump against vodka, single-barrel and small-batch bottlings (Blanton's, 1984) relaunched bourbon as a premium spirit, culminating in today's allocated releases, Pappy Van Winkle mania, and record Kentucky tourism.
production: Distillers cook a mash of corn with a flavoring grain — rye for spice, wheat for softness — plus malted barley for enzymes, usually adding acidic spent stillage ('sour mash') for consistency. Fermentation takes three to five days; distillation typically runs through a column beer still and a pot-style doubler. The clear distillate enters new white-oak barrels charred to specification (commonly #3 or #4 'alligator' char) at 125 proof or less. In multi-story rickhouses, hot upper floors age whiskey fast and cool lower floors slowly, so barrels are batched across positions or bottled singly. There is no minimum age for bourbon as such; 'straight' requires two years, and bottled-in-bond requires four years, 100 proof, and a single distillery and season.

styles.name: Straight bourbon
styles.description: Aged at least two years with no coloring or flavoring; under four years, the label must state the age.

styles.name: Bottled-in-Bond
styles.description: One distillery, one distilling season, at least four years in a bonded warehouse, bottled at exactly 100 proof under the 1897 Act.

styles.name: Wheated bourbon
styles.description: Wheat replaces rye as the flavoring grain for a softer, rounder profile — the Maker's Mark, Weller, and Pappy Van Winkle school.

styles.name: High-rye bourbon
styles.description: A rye-heavy mash bill (roughly 20–35%) adds pepper and dryness — Four Roses and Old Grand-Dad territory.

styles.name: Single barrel
styles.description: Bottled from one barrel at a time, embracing barrel-to-barrel variation; Blanton's pioneered the format in 1984.

styles.name: Barrel proof
styles.description: Bottled uncut at cask strength, often 55–70% ABV — George T. Stagg and Booker's are the archetypes.

funFacts: Aging bourbon barrels outnumber Kentucky's human population roughly two to one.
Because every bourbon barrel is used once, the used casks flow onward to age Scotch, Irish whiskey, rum, and tequila worldwide.
The 1897 Bottled-in-Bond Act was among America's first consumer-protection laws, predating the Pure Food and Drug Act by nine years.

name: Rye Whiskey
tagline: The spicier American original — pre-Prohibition king, reborn behind the bar.
overview: American rye whiskey mirrors bourbon's rulebook with one swap: the mash must be at least 51% rye grain, with the same limits on distillation proof, barrel entry, and new charred oak, and the same 'straight' designation after two years. Rye trades bourbon's sweetness for pepper, grass, mint, and baking spice — a drier, sharper profile that stands up in cocktails, which is why the Sazerac, Manhattan, and original old fashioned were built on it. Note the trap: in Canada, 'rye' is a legal synonym for Canadian whisky generally and implies no minimum rye content. Once America's dominant whiskey, rye nearly vanished by the 1980s; the craft-cocktail renaissance resurrected it, and it has been the fastest-growing American whiskey segment of the past two decades.
history: Rye thrived where the grain did: Pennsylvania and Maryland, where German and Scots-Irish farmer-distillers created the Monongahela and Maryland styles in the 1700s. The 1791 federal excise tax on it sparked the Whiskey Rebellion; George Washington himself ran one of the young republic's largest rye distilleries at Mount Vernon, producing nearly 11,000 gallons in 1799. Rye was America's leading whiskey into Prohibition, which — followed by shifting postwar tastes toward lighter spirits — destroyed it; by the 1990s only a handful of brands like Old Overholt and Wild Turkey Rye survived. Bartenders reviving pre-Prohibition recipes in the 2000s demanded proper rye, and distillers, craft and major alike, rebuilt the category.
production: Rye is a notoriously difficult brewing grain — its beta-glucans make mashes viscous, sticky, and prone to foaming — so distillers earn their spice. Mash bills range from 'barely legal' 51% rye softened with corn (the Kentucky style) through the 95/5 rye-to-malted-barley recipe made famous by Indiana's MGP distillery, to 100% and even fully malted rye. Distillation and maturation follow bourbon practice: column-and-doubler distillation, new charred oak, entry at 125 proof or less. Bottled-in-bond ryes such as Rittenhouse remain bartenders' workhorses for their guaranteed 100 proof. Canadian distillers treat rye differently — distilling it separately, often unaged-grain-forward, as a flavoring whisky for blends.

styles.name: Straight rye
styles.description: The federal standard: 51%+ rye, new charred oak, two years minimum for 'straight' — pepper and spice guaranteed.

styles.name: Monongahela / Pennsylvania
styles.description: The historic high-rye style of western Pennsylvania — oily, intense, grain-forward; craft distillers are resurrecting it.

styles.name: Maryland style
styles.description: The softer, fruitier historical school of rye, often gentled with more corn; another revival target.

styles.name: Kentucky 'barely legal'
styles.description: 51–53% rye with generous corn — spice layered over bourbon-like sweetness (Rittenhouse, Sazerac Rye).

styles.name: 95/5 Indiana rye
styles.description: MGP of Indiana's 95% rye, 5% malted barley recipe — dill, mint, and pepper — the backbone of many sourced brands like early WhistlePig.

styles.name: 100% and malted rye
styles.description: Uncompromising all-rye mashes, sometimes fully malted, showing the grain's dark-bread depth (Alberta-style, Old Potrero).

funFacts: George Washington's reconstructed Mount Vernon distillery once again makes rye whiskey to the 1799 recipe.
A 'rye' label in Canada carries no rye-percentage requirement — tradition, not mash bill, earns the name there.
The Whiskey Rebellion of 1791–94, America's first great tax revolt, was fought largely by rye distillers.

name: American Whiskey
tagline: Beyond bourbon and rye — Tennessee charcoal, new single malts, and corn whiskey.
overview: American whiskey beyond bourbon and rye is a family of distinct legal categories. Tennessee whiskey is essentially bourbon made in Tennessee and filtered through sugar-maple charcoal before barreling — the Lincoln County Process, written into state law in 2013 and exemplified by Jack Daniel's. American single malt, formalized by a federal standard that took effect in January 2025, requires 100% malted barley mashed, fermented, and distilled at a single US distillery and aged in oak casks of 700 liters or less. Corn whiskey (at least 80% corn, aged — if at all — in used or uncharred oak) preserves the frontier's unaged tradition, alongside wheat whiskey, malt whiskey, and blended American whiskey. Together they show US distilling's range beyond its two flagship styles.
history: Charcoal mellowing emerged in early-1800s Tennessee; modern research restored credit to Nearest Green, an enslaved distiller who taught the young Jack Daniel and whose story Brown-Forman formally embraced in 2016–17. Corn whiskey and moonshine carried Appalachian distilling through Prohibition, while blended American whiskey ruled mid-century bars before brown spirits slumped in the 1970s. The craft-distilling boom that began in the 2000s — Stranahan's, Westland, Balcones — bet on American single malt, and the American Single Malt Whiskey Commission spent nearly a decade lobbying until the TTB finalized the category in December 2024, the first new major US whiskey type in generations.
production: Tennessee whiskey is made to bourbon specifications, then the new-make spirit drips through roughly ten feet of packed sugar-maple charcoal over days before entering new charred oak — a subtractive step that strips harsher congeners (George Dickel chills the spirit first; Benjamin Prichard's holds a grandfathered exemption). American single malt mashes only malted barley, is usually pot-distilled, and enjoys wide cask freedom — new charred, used bourbon, ex-wine — since the standard requires oak but not new oak. Corn whiskey may be bottled unaged or rested in used or uncharred barrels, keeping sweet-corn character. Blended American whiskey combines straight whiskey with lighter whiskies or neutral spirit.

styles.name: Tennessee whiskey
styles.description: Bourbon-spec whiskey made in Tennessee and charcoal-mellowed before aging — Jack Daniel's and George Dickel define it.

styles.name: American single malt
styles.description: 100% malted barley from one US distillery; a federal category since January 2025, led by pioneers like Westland and Stranahan's.

styles.name: Corn whiskey
styles.description: At least 80% corn and no new charred oak — the legal heir of frontier and moonshine traditions.

styles.name: Wheat whiskey
styles.description: A 51%+ wheat mash (distinct from wheated bourbon) — soft, bready, and gentle; Bernheim is the standard-bearer.

styles.name: Blended American whiskey
styles.description: Straight whiskey blended with lighter whiskey or grain neutral spirit — the smooth mid-century style of Seagram's 7 Crown.

funFacts: Jack Daniel learned distilling from Nearest Green, an enslaved man later hired as his first master distiller — now honored by the Uncle Nearest brand.
American single malt became the first new federally defined US whiskey category in decades when the rule took effect in January 2025.
Tennessee law lets Benjamin Prichard's skip the charcoal mellowing that every other 'Tennessee whiskey' must perform.

name: Scotch Whisky
tagline: Five regions, one law — malt, oak, and at least three years in Scottish air.
overview: Scotch whisky must be mashed, fermented, distilled, and matured entirely in Scotland, aged at least three years in oak casks no larger than 700 liters, and bottled at 40% ABV or more, with only water and plain caramel coloring (E150a) permitted additions. The Scotch Whisky Regulations 2009 define five categories: single malt (100% malted barley, pot stills, one distillery), single grain, blended malt, blended grain, and blended Scotch — blends still account for the large majority of bottles sold worldwide. Five recognized regions — Speyside, Highlands, Lowlands, Islay, and Campbeltown — offer a stylistic map from orchard-fruited and sherried through grassy to intensely peated and maritime, though distillery character now often outruns regional stereotype.
history: The first written record is the 1494 Exchequer Roll noting malt sold to Friar John Cor 'to make aqua vitae.' Centuries of excise duty bred moonshining and smuggling until the 1823 Excise Act made licensed distilling viable — The Glenlivet took an early license in 1824. Aeneas Coffey's continuous still (1830) enabled cheap grain whisky, and Victorian blenders — Walker, Dewar, Buchanan — built global brands, aided when phylloxera crippled French brandy in the 1870s–80s. The Pattison crash of 1898 and 20th-century consolidation followed; Glenfiddich's export push from 1963 created the modern single-malt market. After the 1980s 'whisky loch' closed distilleries like Port Ellen, today more than 140 operate amid a global boom.
production: Malted barley — kilned over peat smoke for smoky styles, with phenols measured in ppm — is mashed, and the sugary wort ferments into a wash of about 8% ABV. Copper pot stills distill it twice (occasionally two-and-a-half times at Springbank or three at Auchentoshan), with the still's size and shape sculpting spirit weight. New make fills mostly ex-bourbon barrels and hogsheads or ex-sherry butts, maturing in Scotland for a legal minimum of three years and losing around 2% a year to the angels. Single malts are typically married from many casks — age-stated by the youngest — while master blenders assemble blended Scotch from malt and grain whiskies across dozens of distilleries.

styles.name: Speyside
styles.description: Nearly half of Scotland's distilleries along the River Spey — elegant, orchard-fruity, and often sherried (Glenfiddich, Macallan).

styles.name: Highlands
styles.description: The largest, most varied region — heathery, honeyed, sometimes coastal or lightly smoky (Glenmorangie, Oban).

styles.name: Lowlands
styles.description: Historically triple-distilled, light, and grassy — the aperitif malts of Auchentoshan and Glenkinchie.

styles.name: Islay
styles.description: The peat island: medicinal smoke, iodine, and sea spray from Laphroaig, Ardbeg, and Lagavulin.

styles.name: Campbeltown
styles.description: Once 'the whisky capital of the world' with 30+ distilleries, now three — oily, briny, faintly funky malts like Springbank.

styles.name: Single malt vs blended
styles.description: Single malts express one distillery's pot-still barley spirit; blended Scotch marries malts with lighter grain whisky for balance and scale — the format that built the industry.

funFacts: Around 22 million casks are maturing in Scotland at any moment — several for every resident.
The 1494 tax record's eight bolls of malt could have made roughly 1,500 bottles of spirit.
Blends made Scotch global: even today most Scotch sold worldwide is blended, not single malt.

name: Irish Whiskey
tagline: Triple-distilled smoothness and the single pot still revival.
overview: Irish whiskey must be distilled and matured on the island of Ireland (Republic or Northern Ireland) for at least three years in wooden casks of 700 liters or less, bottled at 40% ABV minimum. Its four categories are single malt, single grain, blended — and the style Ireland alone makes: single pot still, distilled from a mash mixing malted with unmalted 'green' barley, which lends a signature creamy texture and peppery spice (Redbreast, Green Spot). Triple distillation is common though not legally required, and peat is rare, yielding a typically smooth, fruity, approachable spirit. Long the world's best-selling whiskey, then nearly extinct by the 1970s, Irish whiskey has been among the fastest-growing spirits categories of the 21st century, with Jameson towering over the field.
history: Ireland's claim to whiskey's origin is ancient — the Annals of Clonmacnoise record a chieftain's death in 1405 from 'a surfeit of aqua vitae' — and Bushmills holds a 1608 license granted for the surrounding district. Nineteenth-century Dublin distillers were the world's largest, and the 1785 malt tax pushed them to mix unmalted barley into the mash, accidentally inventing single pot still whiskey. Decline came brutally: rejection of the Coffey still, Irish independence and the Anglo-Irish trade war, and US Prohibition shrank roughly thirty distilleries to two companies, merged as Irish Distillers by 1966 and concentrated at Midleton in 1975. Cooley's 1987 founding began the revival; Ireland has gone from four operating distilleries in 2013 to more than forty today.
production: Single pot still whiskey mashes malted and unmalted barley together (regulations require at least 30% of each), the raw barley delivering the oily, spicy mouthfeel the style is famous for; Midleton's enormous pot stills triple-distill it into a notably clean, fruity spirit. Single malts follow the Scottish pattern, in both double- and triple-distilled forms, and column stills produce grain whiskey for blends like Jameson, which marry pot still and grain components. Maturation leans on ex-bourbon barrels with sherry and increasingly varied wine casks; the law's 'wooden casks' wording — rather than strictly oak — has also opened the door to chestnut and other experimental woods.

styles.name: Single pot still
styles.description: Ireland's own style: mixed malted and unmalted barley in copper pots — creamy, spicy, and revived from near-extinction by Redbreast and the 'Spot' range.

styles.name: Single malt
styles.description: All-malted-barley whiskey from one distillery — Bushmills has made it continuously longer than almost anyone.

styles.name: Single grain
styles.description: Light column-still whiskey from corn or wheat with malted barley, occasionally bottled solo (Kilbeggan, Teeling).

styles.name: Blended Irish
styles.description: Pot still or malt married with grain whiskey — the smooth, global style of Jameson, Tullamore D.E.W., and Powers.

styles.name: Poitín
styles.description: The unaged 'mountain dew' ancestor, illegal from 1661 to 1997 and now an EU-recognized geographical indication.

funFacts: The 1405 Annals of Clonmacnoise contain whiskey's grimmest early citation: a chieftain who died of 'a surfeit of aqua vitae.'
Dublin's 19th-century distillers popularized the 'e' in whiskey partly to distinguish their product from Scotch.
Bushmills' famous 1608 date refers to a license to distill granted for the whole territory — the company itself was registered in 1784.

name: Japanese Whisky
tagline: Scottish craft through a Japanese lens — harmony, highballs, and mizunara oak.
overview: Japanese whisky began as a faithful study of Scotch and evolved into its own school, prizing balance, texture, and blending artistry. Because rival houses historically refuse to trade casks, each company builds internal variety — Suntory's Yamazaki alone runs pot stills of wildly different shapes — and blends like Hibiki are the category's crown jewels. Native mizunara oak contributes sandalwood, incense, and coconut notes found nowhere else. For decades 'Japanese whisky' had no legal definition, letting imported bulk whisky wear the label; the Japan Spirits & Liqueurs Makers Association's 2021 standard (fully in force from April 2024) now requires fermentation, distillation, three years' cask maturation, and bottling at 40%+ all within Japan. Global acclaim since the mid-2010s stripped shelves of age-stated stock and made top bottlings allocated treasures.
history: Shinjiro Torii opened Japan's first malt whisky distillery at Yamazaki in 1923, hiring Masataka Taketsuru, who had studied chemistry in Glasgow and apprenticed at Scottish distilleries — and returned with a Scottish wife, Rita. Taketsuru left in 1934 to found Nikka at Yoichi on Hokkaido, establishing the Suntory–Nikka rivalry that still defines the industry. Whisky boomed domestically after the war, peaked in the 1980s, then slid for two decades until Suntory's 2008 kaku-highball campaign revived it. International awards — capped when Jim Murray named Yamazaki Sherry Cask 2013 the world's best in his 2015 guide — ignited global demand, shortages, and finally the 2021 labeling standards.
production: The model is Scottish — malted barley (much of it imported, some peated), copper pot stills, careful cuts — but deployed for in-house diversity: multiple still shapes, yeast strains, and fermentation regimes at a single site yield dozens of component styles. Grain whisky comes from column and Coffey stills (Nikka's Coffey Grain made the still famous again). Maturation uses ex-bourbon, sherry, and wine casks plus mizunara (Quercus crispula), a porous, leak-prone oak that must grow around two centuries before coopering and rewards patience with temple-incense aromatics. Blenders assemble the results with a precision the industry regards as its defining craft, and the 2021 rules bind the 'Japanese whisky' label to fully domestic production.

styles.name: Single malt
styles.description: One distillery's pot-still malt — Yamazaki's layered fruit, Hakushu's forest-green freshness, Yoichi's coal-fired muscle.

styles.name: Blended
styles.description: The house art form: malt and grain married for seamlessness — Hibiki is the standard-bearer.

styles.name: Coffey grain
styles.description: Whisky from vintage Coffey continuous stills, sweet and silky; a Nikka signature.

styles.name: Mizunara cask
styles.description: Whisky aged in rare Japanese oak, giving sandalwood, kara incense, and coconut — expensive and leak-prone, but inimitable.

styles.name: Peated Japanese
styles.description: Smoky malts in the Islay vein from Yoichi and Hakushu's peated runs — restrained, saline, precise.

funFacts: Mizunara oaks are typically around 200 years old before they're large and straight enough to cooper.
Suntory's 2008 highball campaign — whisky, sparkling water, a lemon twist — is credited with rescuing Japanese whisky's domestic market.
Before the 2021 standards, some 'Japanese whisky' was legally bulk Scotch or Canadian whisky bottled in Japan; the loophole closed fully in April 2024.

name: Canadian Whisky
tagline: The blender's whisky — rye by name, silky by design.
overview: Canadian whisky must be mashed, distilled, and aged in Canada for at least three years in small wood (casks of 700 liters or less) and bottled at 40% ABV minimum; caramel and flavoring are permitted, and 'Canadian whisky,' 'Canadian rye whisky,' and 'rye whisky' are legal synonyms regardless of how much rye the mash contains. The defining method is component blending: distillers make a high-proof, near-neutral 'base whisky' and lower-proof, characterful 'flavoring whiskies' from individual grains, age them separately, and blend afterward — yielding the smooth, light, mixable house style that made Canadian whisky North America's volume leader for generations. A famous rule allows up to 9.09% (one-eleventh) of other aged spirits or wine in blends. Serious 100% rye bottlings like Lot No. 40 have led a critical reappraisal.
history: Milling towns of Upper Canada birthed the industry — Gooderham & Worts in Toronto, Hiram Walker's 1858 distillery at Walkerville, J.P. Wiser's in Prescott. German and Dutch settlers' taste for rye bread led farmers to add rye to wheat mashes, and 'rye' became the national shorthand. American upheavals were Canada's windfalls: Civil War disruption opened US markets, and Prohibition (1920–33) enriched exporters — legally distilling, however the goods crossed the border — vaulting the Bronfmans' Seagram empire to world dominance. Crown Royal was created for King George VI's 1939 royal train tour. Decades of decline as light spirits faded were followed by a 2010s revival built on premium ryes and single-cask releases.
production: Grains are handled separately rather than in one mash bill: corn or wheat is column-distilled above 90% ABV into base whisky and aged in used casks for gentleness, while rye (plus some corn and barley) is distilled at lower proof in pots or short columns into intensely flavored flavoring whisky, often aged in new or ex-bourbon oak. After a minimum three years, blenders combine base and flavoring whiskies — the flavoring share may be modest, which is why many Canadian whiskies taste delicate despite the 'rye' name. The 9.09% allowance permits blending in other spirits or wine, mostly used for export recipes. Alberta Distillers, unusually, mashes 100% rye at industrial scale.

styles.name: Classic blended Canadian
styles.description: Base-plus-flavoring blends — Crown Royal, Canadian Club, Seagram's VO — light, creamy, built for highballs.

styles.name: 100% rye
styles.description: All-rye whiskies like Lot No. 40 and Alberta Premium: baking spice, dark bread, and proof that Canada can do intensity.

styles.name: Pot-still flavoring whisky
styles.description: The low-proof, high-character component whiskies, occasionally bottled straight for enthusiasts.

styles.name: Premium and cask-strength releases
styles.description: Age-stated, single-cask, and barrel-proof bottlings (Crown Royal Northern Harvest, Canadian Club Chronicles) driving the modern reappraisal.

funFacts: Crown Royal was blended in 1939 specifically for the first royal tour of Canada by a reigning monarch, King George VI.
A Canadian 'rye' can legally contain little or no rye grain — the name honors tradition, not a recipe.
During US Prohibition, Canadian distilleries operated legally and boomed as their whisky mysteriously kept reaching American drinkers.

name: World Whisky
tagline: Whisky without borders — tropical aging, new grains, and novel terroirs.
overview: World whisky gathers everything beyond the five classic nations, and it is where the category experiments hardest. Taiwan's Kavalan proved subtropical heat can mature malt astonishingly fast — evaporation runs around 10% or more a year, so a five-year whisky drinks like one far older. India's Amrut and Paul John did the same in Bangalore and Goa (most domestic 'Indian whisky,' by contrast, is molasses-based and doesn't qualify as whisky in the EU). Tasmania rebooted Australian distilling; Iceland's Eimverk grows its own barley for Flóki and smokes some of it over sheep dung; Sweden, France, England, and Wales all field serious malts. Once curiosities, world whiskies now take top global awards routinely.
history: Pioneers worked in isolation: Bill Lark relicensed Tasmanian distilling in 1992, ending a ban dating to 1838; Amrut began its single-malt project in the 1980s and launched in Glasgow in 2004; Penderyn returned whisky-making to Wales in the 2000s; Kavalan was founded in 2005 and bottled its first single malt in 2008, guided by the late maturation scientist Dr. Jim Swan. The tipping points came fast: Sullivans Cove's French Oak was named World's Best Single Malt in 2014, Kavalan's Solist Vinho Barrique took the same title in 2015, and Indian single malts began outselling Scotch brands at home. Today essentially every continent except Antarctica distills whisky.
production: The fundamentals are Scottish — malted barley, pot stills, oak — reshaped by climate and ingenuity. Tropical and subtropical warehouses multiply both extraction and evaporation, compressing decades of maturation into a few years at the cost of huge angel's shares. Dr. Jim Swan's STR casks (shaved, toasted, re-charred ex-wine barrels) became the fast-maturation workhorse from Taiwan to Wales. Local raw materials define house styles: India's six-row barley, Iceland's short-season organic barley and geothermal energy, Australia's ex-fortified 'Apera' casks, and smoke sources from sheep dung to native woods replacing peat where none exists.

styles.name: Taiwanese
styles.description: Kavalan's lush, fast-matured malts — tropical fruit and dense cask influence from ex-wine and sherry wood.

styles.name: Indian single malt
styles.description: Amrut, Paul John, and Rampur: jaggery-sweet, spicy malts matured at Indian speed — and a booming domestic premium market.

styles.name: Australian
styles.description: Tasmania-led malts in ex-fortified casks — Sullivans Cove, Lark — rich, winey, and small-scale.

styles.name: Nordic and Icelandic
styles.description: Mackmyra's Swedish forest-and-juniper experiments and Eimverk's Flóki, a young, grain-forward malt from 100% Icelandic barley.

styles.name: English and Welsh
styles.description: The revival wing — Penderyn's Faraday-still malts and the English Whisky Co. and Cotswolds' fast-rising single malts.

funFacts: Kavalan went from breaking ground in 2005 to a World's Best Single Malt award within a decade.
Flóki smokes some of its barley over dried sheep dung — Iceland's traditional fuel in a nearly treeless landscape.
India consumes about half the world's whisky by volume, though most of it is molasses-based spirit that can't be labeled whisky in Europe.

name: Tequila
tagline: Blue agave under Mexican law — from tahona-crushed blancos to oak-lush extra añejos.
overview: Tequila is a denomination-of-origin spirit made only from blue Weber agave (Agave tequilana) in Jalisco and designated municipalities of Guanajuato, Michoacán, Nayarit, and Tamaulipas, regulated by the CRT under Mexico's NOM standard. '100% de agave' tequila must be fermented purely from agave sugars and bottled in Mexico; 'mixto' needs only 51%. Aging defines the classes: blanco (under two months), reposado (two months to a year), añejo (one to three years in casks of 600 liters or less), extra añejo (three-plus years), plus joven blends and charcoal-filtered cristalinos. Terroir splits the map — highland (Los Altos) agaves lean sweet and floral, valley agaves earthy and peppery. Mexican rules allow up to 1% additives without labeling, which sparked the additive-free verification movement.
history: Distillation of the region's 'vino de mezcal' grew through the colonial era, and the town of Tequila industrialized it — the Cuervo family received a royal license in 1795, and Cenobio Sauza began exporting north in the 1870s. The margarita's mid-20th-century rise made tequila an American bar staple. Mexico created the tequila denomination of origin in 1974, among the first outside Europe, and the CRT (1994) formalized enforcement. The 1990s–2000s saw 100% agave premiumization led by brands like Patrón and Fortaleza's tahona revival; the 2010s added a celebrity gold rush, cristalinos, boom-and-bust agave price cycles, and heated debate over additives and diffuser-made tequila.
production: Blue agaves mature six to eight years before jimadores harvest and trim the piñas. Cooking converts starches to sugar: slow steam in brick ovens, faster autoclaves, or — at industrial scale — diffusers that strip sugars from shredded raw fiber, a shortcut critics say hollows out flavor. Roller mills or a stone tahona wheel crush the cooked agave; fermentation, sometimes with fibers, runs in wood or steel; double distillation (typically copper or stainless) yields spirit around 55%. Aging happens mostly in ex-bourbon barrels. Every bottle carries the NOM number of its distillery, and the CRT audits fields, sugars, and cellars throughout.

styles.name: Blanco
styles.description: Unaged (or under two months) — the purest read on cooked agave: citrus, pepper, and minerality.

styles.name: Reposado
styles.description: Rested two months to a year in oak; vanilla and soft spice folded over agave.

styles.name: Añejo
styles.description: One to three years in small casks — caramel, dried fruit, and bourbon-adjacent depth.

styles.name: Extra añejo
styles.description: Three years or more, a class created in 2006; tequila's answer to XO cognac.

styles.name: Cristalino
styles.description: Aged tequila charcoal-filtered back to clarity — sweet, polished, and popular in Mexico's luxury market.

styles.name: Mixto
styles.description: The 51% agave minimum, usually bottled young for shots and well drinks; what '100% de agave' labels exist to disavow.

funFacts: The four-digit NOM number on every label identifies the actual distillery — many brands share the same stills.
The agave landscape around Tequila and its historic distilleries is a UNESCO World Heritage site (2006).
Because blue agave takes the better part of a decade to mature, the industry swings through recurring shortage-and-glut price cycles.

name: Mezcal
tagline: Smoke, clay, and wild agaves — Oaxaca's village spirit gone global.
overview: Mezcal is the broad traditional family of Mexican agave spirits, protected by a 1994 denomination of origin now spanning nine-plus states, with Oaxaca producing the overwhelming majority. Unlike tequila's single species, mezcal may use dozens of permitted agaves: cultivated espadín accounts for most production, while wild and semi-wild tobalá, tepeztate, arroqueño, and madrecuixe command cult prices. Its signature smokiness comes from roasting piñas in wood-fired earthen pits, though smoke is a byproduct, not the point — species, village, water, and the mezcalero's hand matter more. The NOM-070 standard grades production technology: industrial 'Mezcal,' traditional 'Artesanal,' and clay-pot 'Ancestral.' Most is bottled joven (unaged), sipped neat — traditionally with orange slices and sal de gusano.
history: The word comes from the Nahuatl mexcalli, 'oven-cooked agave.' Whether distillation predates the conquest is a live scholarly debate — Filipino-style stills arrived on colonial Pacific trade routes in the 16th–17th centuries, and clay-pot traditions may be older. For centuries mezcal was village economy and ritual, periodically taxed or banned, and often clandestine. The famous worm was a 1940s–50s marketing flourish credited to bottler Jacobo Lozano Páez.
