The 1994 DO and 2016's NOM-070 categories formalized the industry just as single-village bottlings — pioneered by Del Maguey from 1995 — converted bartenders worldwide, bringing export booms and hard questions about agave sustainability and who profits.
production: Agave hearts — espadín after seven-odd years, tepeztate after up to three decades — are roasted for days in conical earthen pits over hot river stones and hardwood, caramelizing sugars and kissing them with smoke. The cooked piñas are crushed by horse-drawn tahona, mechanical mill, or hand mallet, then fermented open to wild yeasts in wooden tinas, rawhide, or clay. Distillation runs twice through small copper alembics (artesanal) or clay pots with bamboo fittings (ancestral) at family palenques. Mezcaleros adjust the final proof by taste and by reading the perlas — the bead of bubbles a pour raises — rather than by lab instruments alone.

styles.name: Espadín
styles.description: The workhorse cultivated agave behind most mezcal — generous, fruity, and the best value in the category.

styles.name: Tobalá
styles.description: A small, prized wild mountain agave giving perfumed, delicate spirit in tiny yields.

styles.name: Tepeztate
styles.description: A cliff-growing wild agave that can take 25–35 years to mature — wildly vegetal, peppery, and rare.

styles.name: Ensamble
styles.description: Two or more agave species roasted and fermented together into a single, layered spirit.

styles.name: Pechuga
styles.description: Mezcal redistilled with fruits, spices, and traditionally a raw chicken or turkey breast hung in the still — a celebration spirit.

styles.name: Ancestral clay-pot
styles.description: Distilled in clay ollas as in centuries past — earthy, textural, and NOM-070's most traditional class.

funFacts: Mezcaleros judge proof by the perlas — the size and persistence of bubbles when spirit is poured — a technique older than hydrometers.
The bottle worm (a gusano moth larva that feeds on agave) was pure mid-century marketing, and quality producers skip it.
An Oaxacan proverb covers all cases: 'Para todo mal, mezcal; para todo bien, también' — for everything bad, mezcal; for everything good, the same.

name: Cognac
tagline: Twice-distilled white wine from Charente chalk — the world's benchmark brandy.
overview: Cognac is AOC grape brandy from a delimited region around the town of Cognac in western France, distilled twice in Charentais copper pot stills and aged in French oak. Ugni blanc supplies roughly 98% of the base wine, prized for high acid and low alcohol. Six crus ring the town: Grande Champagne and Petite Champagne on chalky soils yield the finest, longest-aging eaux-de-vie ('Fine Champagne' blends the two with at least half Grande Champagne), followed by Borderies, Fins Bois, Bons Bois, and Bois Ordinaires. Age statements count the youngest component: VS at least two years, VSOP four, Napoléon six, XO ten (raised from six in 2018), and XXO fourteen. About 98% of cognac is exported, and four big houses dominate global sales.
history: Dutch traders distilling Charente wine for shipboard durability created 'brandwijn' in the 16th–17th centuries; double distillation and cask aging refined it into cognac, and the great houses arrived in the 1700s — Martell (1715), Rémy Martin (1724), Hennessy (1765). The 19th century brought golden-age exports, then catastrophe: phylloxera arrived in the 1870s and destroyed the vineyards, which were replanted with ugni blanc grafted on American rootstock, displacing folle blanche. The region was legally delimited in 1909 and the AOC framework followed in the 1930s. Postwar, cognac cemented itself as a global luxury; American hip-hop culture revitalized US sales from the 1990s, and Asian markets drive the modern boom-bust cycles.
production: High-acid ugni blanc wine of around 9% ABV, unsulfured, is distilled twice by tradition and law in copper Charentais alembics: a first pass yields brouillis around 30%, and the second — the bonne chauffe, with head and tail cuts — a clear eau-de-vie near 70%. Distillation must finish by March 31 after harvest. The spirit ages in 270–450-liter Limousin or Tronçais oak, new wood first for extraction, older casks for patience; humid cellars soften, dry cellars strengthen. Cellar masters (maîtres de chai) blend dozens or hundreds of eaux-de-vie across ages and crus, easing proof down gradually toward 40%, with the oldest reserves resting in a cellar traditionally called le paradis.

styles.name: VS
styles.description: Youngest component at least two years old — bright, grapey, and the workhorse for mixing (Hennessy V.S leads the world).

styles.name: VSOP
styles.description: 'Very Superior Old Pale,' minimum four years — rounder, with vanilla and dried fruit; the category's center of gravity.

styles.name: XO
styles.description: Minimum ten years since 2018 — rancio, spice, and polish; the prestige tier for most houses.

styles.name: XXO and Hors d'âge
styles.description: Fourteen years and (usually far) beyond — decades-old blends where rancio, leather, and exotic fruit take over.

styles.name: Fine Champagne
styles.description: A blend exclusively of Grande and Petite Champagne eaux-de-vie, at least 50% Grande — Rémy Martin's signature.

styles.name: Single-estate and vintage
styles.description: Grower cognacs and rare dated bottlings that trade house consistency for terroir and year character.

funFacts: The black fungus staining Cognac's cellar walls, Baudoinia compniacensis, literally feeds on the angel's share of evaporating spirit.
Cognac stops aging the moment it's bottled — a 'younger' XO kept 50 years in glass is still an XO.
The 'Champagne' in Grande and Petite Champagne shares a root with the sparkling-wine region: both describe open chalky country.

name: Armagnac
tagline: Gascony's rustic elder — single-distilled, vintage-dated, farm-scale.
overview: Armagnac is Gascony's AOC grape brandy and France's oldest documented spirit, made in three zones southeast of Bordeaux: Bas-Armagnac, whose sandy 'sables fauves' soils give the most refined, fruit-driven eaux-de-vie; clay-limestone Ténarèze, structured and slow to mature; and the marginal Haut-Armagnac. Permitted grapes include ugni blanc, folle blanche, colombard, and baco 22A — a hybrid, uniquely tolerated in French appellation law. The hallmark is single continuous distillation to just 52–60% ABV in the small alambic armagnacais, which keeps far more congeners than cognac's double distillation: armagnac is the earthier, more rustic cousin, and single-vintage bottlings are a specialty. Production remains a fraction of cognac's, spread across hundreds of small family estates.
history: A 1310 text by Vital du Four, prior of Eauze, credited the region's 'aygue ardente' with forty virtues — the earliest substantial French account of a distilled spirit, predating cognac's rise by centuries. Armagnac traded down Gascony's rivers to Bordeaux for export, always overshadowed by its coastal rival's shipping advantage. The distinctive small column still spread through the 19th century and was consecrated in regulation; phylloxera devastated the vineyards in the 1870s, after which baco 22A was bred for the sandy soils. The AOC dates to 1936, double distillation was re-authorized in 1972, and the unaged Blanche d'Armagnac gained its own AOC in 2005. It remains defiantly artisan.
production: White wine of roughly 8.5–10.5% ABV, made without sulfur, passes once through the alambic armagnacais — a small copper continuous still — emerging at only 52–60% ABV, dense with fruit and congeners that demand long aging (some producers use pot stills, permitted again since 1972). The spirit matures in 400–420-liter casks, traditionally of local Gascon oak, moving from new to older wood; many estates bottle single vintages at natural cask strength (brut de fût) after decades. Mobile stills — alambics ambulants — still tour from farm to farm each winter, distilling for growers without their own equipment. Blends carry VS (about one to three years), VSOP (four-plus), and XO/Hors d'âge (ten-plus) designations.

styles.name: Bas-Armagnac
styles.description: The westernmost and most prestigious zone — sandy soils, delicate plum-and-prune fruit, the source of most top single estates.

styles.name: Armagnac-Ténarèze
styles.description: Bolder, more tannic eaux-de-vie from clay-limestone that reward very long aging.

styles.name: Vintage (millésime)
styles.description: Single-year armagnacs, often bottled at cask strength after decades — a birth-year gift tradition cognac rarely matches.

styles.name: XO and Hors d'âge
styles.description: Blends at least ten years old, typically far older — rancio, orange peel, and forest-floor depth.

styles.name: Blanche d'Armagnac
styles.description: The unaged white eau-de-vie, AOC since 2005 — floral, grapey, and increasingly a cocktail spirit.

funFacts: Vital du Four's 1310 list of armagnac's forty virtues includes sharpening wit and 'recalling the forgotten to memory.'
Traveling alambics ambulants still crisscross Gascony each winter, distilling in farmyards as they have for two centuries.
Baco 22A is the only hybrid grape sanctioned in a French appellation — bred after phylloxera specifically for Bas-Armagnac's sands.

name: Calvados
tagline: Normandy's apple brandy — orchards, cider, and oak instead of vines.
overview: Calvados is Normandy's AOC apple brandy — distilled cider, aged in oak, in a region too cool for wine grapes. Three appellations structure it: the broad AOC Calvados (most production, typically single column distillation, minimum two years in oak); Calvados Pays d'Auge, the heartland east of Caen, which requires double distillation in pot stills; and Calvados Domfrontais, which must contain at least 30% perry pears and age three years, reflecting its pear-orchard country (AOC 1997). Producers blend dozens of authorized apple varieties — bitter, bittersweet, sweet, and sharp, most inedible off the tree — for structured cider worth distilling. Expect baked apple, orchard funk, and warming spice deepening with age from Fine (two years) through VO/VSOP (four) to XO and Hors d'âge (six and beyond).
history: Gilles de Gouberville's estate diary records distilling Norman cider in 1553, and a distillers' guild followed in 1606. The name comes from the Calvados département, created in 1790. When phylloxera throttled France's wine and brandy supply in the late 1800s, apple brandy filled the gap nationally, and soldiers' rations spread the taste during the First World War. The AOC arrived in 1942, during occupation. D-Day put the region at history's center — Allied troops fought through the orchards and met the local spirit. Long a farmhouse digestif, calvados has been rediscovered through cocktail classics like the Jack Rose and by chefs reviving Norman gastronomy.
production: Cider apples are harvested and pressed in autumn, the juice fermenting slowly on wild yeasts for at least six weeks — often through winter — to a dry cider of 4.5–6% ABV (Domfrontais mashes include at least 30% perry pears). Pays d'Auge producers distill twice in copper pot stills for a finer, rounder spirit; elsewhere a single pass through a column still preserves brighter fruit. The brandy ages a legal minimum of two years (three for Domfrontais) in French oak, often large old tuns that season rather than dominate; blenders assemble across ages, while some estates release single vintages. Farm producers (fermier), cooperatives, and grand houses coexist.

styles.name: Pays d'Auge
styles.description: The prestige appellation: double pot distillation from the chalky eastern heartland — elegant, apple-pure, age-worthy.

styles.name: Domfrontais
styles.description: Pear-rich calvados (30%+ perry pears) with a lighter, floral lift; column-distilled, minimum three years.

styles.name: Fine / young calvados
styles.description: Two-to-three-year bottlings with fresh orchard bite — the mixing grade behind the Calvados highball and Jack Rose.

styles.name: XO and Hors d'âge
styles.description: Six years minimum and often decades — baked apple tarte tatin, spice, and rancio.

styles.name: Vintage (millésime)
styles.description: Single-harvest bottlings from estates like Christian Drouin and Lemorton, tracking one autumn's orchards across decades.

funFacts: The trou normand — a mid-feast shot of calvados to 'make a hole' for the next course — survives, now often as calvados over apple sorbet.
Hundreds of authorized cider-apple varieties go into calvados, most too bitter or tannic to eat.
Perry pear trees in the Domfrontais can bear fruit for two centuries or more — some working orchards predate the French Revolution.

name: Red Wine
tagline: Skins-on fermentation — color, tannin, and cellar-worthy structure.
overview: Red wine is fermented in contact with dark grape skins, which surrender anthocyanin pigments and tannins — the flesh of nearly every wine grape is colorless, so maceration is what makes red wine red. Tannin gives the drying grip that structures reds and lets the best of them age for decades, while styles span an enormous arc: pale, perfumed pinot noir and gamay; mid-weight merlot, sangiovese, and tempranillo; and dense cabernet sauvignon, syrah, and malbec. Most reds undergo malolactic conversion, softening acid, and many see oak. Serve them cooler than modern rooms — around 16–18°C — since 'room temperature' advice predates central heating. Tannin's affinity for protein underwrites the classic pairing of red wine with meat and hard cheese.
history: Skin-fermented wine is the ancient default — the qvevri wines of Georgia and amphora wines of Greece and Rome were effectively what we would call reds and ambers. Medieval Bordeaux built England's claret trade after Eleanor of Aquitaine's 1152 marriage; Burgundy's monks mapped pinot noir crus vineyard by vineyard. The 1855 classification ranked Médoc châteaux for Napoleon III's Paris Exposition and has barely changed since. After phylloxera and reconstruction, the modern era brought technical control, the 1976 Judgment of Paris — where Stag's Leap's 1973 cabernet beat first-growth Bordeaux blind — the extraction-heavy 1990s, and today's swing back toward freshness, lighter touch, and cooler climates.
production: Grapes are destemmed (or kept partly whole-cluster for aromatics and structure) and crushed; fermentation on skins runs warm — roughly 20–32°C — for five days to several weeks, with the floating cap of skins punched down or pumped over to manage extraction. The wine is pressed off, completes malolactic conversion, and matures in tank, concrete, or oak barrels (new oak adding spice and toast) for six months to several years. Carbonic maceration — whole berries fermenting internally under CO2, as in Beaujolais — yields juicy, low-tannin reds for early drinking. Fining and filtration are optional finishing choices; many age-worthy reds throw natural sediment instead.

styles.name: Light-bodied
styles.description: Pinot noir, gamay, and trousseau — pale, aromatic, low-tannin reds that reward a slight chill.

styles.name: Medium-bodied
styles.description: Merlot, sangiovese, tempranillo, and cabernet franc — the food-wine middleweight class from Chianti to Rioja.

styles.name: Full-bodied
styles.description: Cabernet sauvignon, syrah/shiraz, malbec, and zinfandel — dark fruit, firm tannin, and oak-framed power.

styles.name: Carbonic and nouveau
styles.description: Whole-berry ferments like Beaujolais Nouveau — bright, banana-and-berry reds released within weeks of harvest.

styles.name: Age-worthy classics
styles.description: Barolo, grand cru Bordeaux and Burgundy, Rioja Gran Reserva, Brunello — wines built on tannin and acid to evolve for decades.

funFacts: Almost all red-wine color comes from skins: press a pinot noir immediately and you get white juice — the trick behind blanc de noirs champagne.
At the 1976 Judgment of Paris, French judges blind-ranked Californian reds and whites above storied French labels, upending wine's hierarchy.
The 1855 Bordeaux classification has seen essentially one major promotion since it was written: Mouton Rothschild's elevation in 1973.

name: White Wine
tagline: Pressed clean, fermented cool — acidity, aroma, and precision.
overview: White wine is made by pressing juice off the skins before fermentation and fermenting it cool — typically 12–22°C — to preserve delicate esters and aromatics. Because grape flesh is colorless, whites can even be made from red grapes (blanc de noirs). Acidity is the style's spine, carrying a spectrum from aromatic riesling, sauvignon blanc, and gewürztraminer through neutral, mineral chablis, muscadet, and pinot grigio to rich, barrel-fermented chardonnay worked on its lees. Sweet whites — botrytized sauternes and tokaji, late-harvest and icewines — are among the longest-lived wines of all, and great German riesling and white Burgundy age for decades. Serving temperature runs roughly 7–13°C, colder for lighter wines, warmer for rich ones.
history: Whites were prized in antiquity and codified by medieval monks — Corton-Charlemagne's white vines come with a legend that Charlemagne's wife wanted his beard unstained. Hungary's Tokaj region classified its sweet-wine vineyards by royal decree in 1737, generations before Bordeaux's 1855 ranking. The transformative modern development was temperature control: stainless-steel tanks and refrigeration from the 1960s let warm regions make fresh, fruit-driven whites, and Marlborough sauvignon blanc — first planted in 1973, propelled by Cloudy Bay from 1985 — became the template for a New World aromatic style. Since then the pendulum has swung between opulent oaked chardonnay and today's leaner, earlier-picked precision.
production: Grapes are pressed quickly and gently — whole-cluster for the finest sparkling and still whites — and the juice settles clear before fermenting cool in stainless steel (for fruit purity) or barrel (for texture and integration). Malolactic conversion is a stylistic switch: encouraged for creamy, buttery chardonnay, blocked to keep riesling and sauvignon zesty. Lees aging and stirring (bâtonnage) build weight, as in muscadet sur lie. Extended skin contact turns white grapes into amber 'orange' wine. Sweetness comes from noble rot concentrating sugars (sauternes, tokaji aszú), berries frozen on the vine for icewine, dried grapes, or arrested fermentation; sulfur and oxygen management guard the fragile aromatics throughout.

styles.name: Aromatic
styles.description: Riesling, sauvignon blanc, gewürztraminer, and muscat — perfume-forward wines from cool sites, dry to lusciously sweet.

styles.name: Neutral and mineral
styles.description: Chablis, muscadet, pinot grigio, and assyrtiko — quiet grapes that transmit site, salinity, and freshness.

styles.name: Rich and oaked
styles.description: Barrel-fermented chardonnay, viognier, and white Rioja — round, creamy wines built on lees work and wood.

styles.name: Sweet and botrytized
styles.description: Sauternes, Tokaji aszú, icewine, and beerenauslese — concentrated nectar wines balanced by piercing acidity.

styles.name: Skin-contact (orange)
styles.description: Whites fermented on skins for days or months — amber, tannic, and ancient, revived from Georgian and Friulian tradition.

funFacts: Tokaj's 1737 royal vineyard classification predates Bordeaux's famous 1855 ranking by more than a century.
Icewine grapes are picked frozen solid — legally at −7°C or below in Germany, −8°C in Canada — often in the middle of the night.
Château d'Yquem's yields are so low that each vine is said to produce roughly one glass of wine per year.

name: Rosé
tagline: Brief skin contact, pale glory — from Provence pallor to Tavel power.
overview: Rosé takes its pink from red grapes handled gently: either pressed directly for the palest wines, macerated on skins for just a few hours, or bled off a red fermentation (saignée), which makes rosé while concentrating the remaining red. Blending finished red and white wine is prohibited for still rosé in the EU — champagne rosé is the sanctioned exception. Provence sets the global benchmark: bone-dry, pale salmon, grenache- and cinsault-based, built for the table and the terrace. Tavel in the Rhône makes only rosé — dark, structured, and gastronomic — while California's off-dry white zinfandel remains a category of its own. Most rosé is drunk young and cold, though Tavel and Bandol can age.
history: Much ancient and medieval wine was effectively rosé — short macerations and field pressing made light 'clairet,' the root of England's claret. Provence, planted by Greek settlers around 600 BC, has leaned pink for most of its history. The modern story has two arcs: sweet postwar exports like Mateus and Lancers, then Sutter Home's 1975 stuck fermentation, which accidentally created sweet white zinfandel and incidentally preserved California's old zinfandel vines. The 2000s brought the Provence-led dry wave — Whispering Angel, 'rosé all day,' celebrity estates — that turned rosé from summer afterthought into a year-round premium category and pushed pale-and-dry as the world's default style.
production: Growers often pick slightly early to keep acidity. Direct pressing yields the palest, most delicate juice; short macerations of roughly 2–24 hours deepen color and red-fruit flavor; saignée rosés, bled from red tanks, are the fullest. Fermentation then proceeds like white wine — cool, usually in stainless steel, occasionally in barrel for gastronomic bottlings. Color is managed obsessively, since the market reads pallor as quality in the Provençal idiom. Most rosé is bottled within months under inert gas and meant for drinking inside two or three years, with structured exceptions from Tavel, Bandol's mourvèdre rosés, and a growing wave of sparkling and pét-nat pinks.

styles.name: Provence dry
styles.description: Pale, crisp grenache-cinsault blends — the global benchmark style from Côtes de Provence.

styles.name: Tavel and Bandol
styles.description: The serious end: Tavel is France's all-rosé appellation, dark and vinous; Bandol adds mourvèdre muscle and age-worthiness.

styles.name: Saignée
styles.description: Rosé bled from red fermentations — deeper color, more body, a byproduct turned specialty.

styles.name: White Zinfandel
styles.description: California's off-dry pink, born by accident in 1975 — long the top-selling US rosé style.

styles.name: Sparkling and pét-nat rosé
styles.description: From champagne rosé (where blending is allowed) to cloudy natural-wine pét-nats.

funFacts: Sutter Home's white zinfandel came from a stuck 1975 fermentation — and its success is credited with saving California's old-vine zinfandel from being ripped out.
Roughly nine in ten bottles produced in Provence are rosé.
EU law bans blending red and white wine to make still rosé; champagne rosé is the famous exception.

name: Champagne
tagline: One region, one method — France's protected sparkling standard-bearer.
overview: Champagne is sparkling wine exclusively from the delimited Champagne AOC in northeastern France — about 34,000 hectares of chalk-soiled vineyards — made by the traditional method, in which a second fermentation inside the very bottle sold creates roughly six bars of pressure. Chardonnay, pinot noir, and meunier dominate (four rare varieties are also permitted). Non-vintage blends across years for house consistency and must age at least 15 months; vintage champagne, from a single declared year, requires three years; prestige cuvées like Dom Pérignon and Cristal crown each house's range. Dryness runs from brut nature to doux, and blanc de blancs, blanc de noirs, grower ('RM') and house ('NM') bottlings map the field. The name is fiercely protected worldwide.
history: Champagne's wines were still for centuries — bubbles were a winter fault. The English documented deliberately re-fermenting wine with sugar in 1662 (Christopher Merret's paper), before Dom Pérignon's tenure at Hautvillers; the monk was a great blender and quality reformer, not the inventor of sparkle. Houses arrived through the 18th century — Ruinart first in 1729 — and Veuve Clicquot's cellar team invented riddling around 1816–18, clarifying the wine at scale. Brut styles emerged for the English market in the 1870s. Vineyard riots in 1911 and delimitation battles produced today's strict boundaries, and the 20th century built champagne into the world's celebration wine, now counterweighted by a thriving grower movement.
production: Hand-harvest is mandatory, and whole clusters are pressed gently under strict yield fractions. Base wines ferment dry, malolactic optional, then the assemblage blends crus, varieties, and — for non-vintage — reserve wines from prior years. Bottled with a dose of yeast and sugar (tirage), the wine referments, gaining about 1.3% alcohol and its CO2, then ages on the lees — 12 months minimum on lees within 15 total for NV, three years for vintage, and far longer at ambitious houses — building brioche and toast through autolysis. Riddling (now largely by gyropalette) coaxes sediment into the neck; the frozen plug is disgorged, dosage liqueur sets the sweetness level, and the cork and cage go on.

styles.name: Non-vintage brut
styles.description: The house calling card: a multi-year blend engineered for consistency, at least 15 months' aging.

styles.name: Vintage
styles.description: A single declared year, aged three years minimum — made only when the harvest merits it.

styles.name: Prestige cuvée
styles.description: The flagship — Dom Pérignon, Cristal, Krug's Grande Cuvée (itself a multi-vintage) — from top crus and long lees aging.

styles.name: Blanc de blancs
styles.description: All-chardonnay champagne, often from the Côte des Blancs — citrus, chalk, and tension.

styles.name: Blanc de noirs
styles.description: White champagne from black grapes (pinot noir, meunier) — broader, red-fruited power.

styles.name: Rosé champagne
styles.description: Made by maceration or, uniquely in the EU, by blending in a little still red wine.

funFacts: Christopher Merret described adding sugar to wine to make it sparkle in 1662 — years before Dom Pérignon arrived at Hautvillers.
Physicist Gérard Liger-Belair calculated that a single flute releases on the order of a million bubbles.
Riddling was devised in Veuve Clicquot's cellars around 1816–18 — lore says the first pupitre was the widow's own kitchen table.

name: Sparkling Wine
tagline: Bubbles by every method — prosecco tanks, cava cellars, pét-nat crown caps.
overview: Sparkling wine outside Champagne spans every price, grape, and method. Prosecco — Glera grapes from Veneto and Friuli — uses the tank (Charmat) method, refermenting in pressurized steel for weeks to keep it fresh, floral, and affordable; its Conegliano Valdobbiadene DOCG hills are a UNESCO site. Cava, mostly from Catalonia's Penedès, follows the champagne playbook with macabeo, xarel·lo, and parellada aged at least nine months on lees. France's crémants, Italy's Franciacorta, and German Sekt fill out the traditional-method family, while pét-nat (méthode ancestrale) — bottled before its first fermentation finishes, often cloudy, under crown cap — is both the oldest technique and the natural-wine era's favorite. Method drives style: tank preserves primary fruit; bottle-and-lees aging builds bready depth.
history: The ancestral method is the oldest — Limoux's Benedictines claim sparkling Blanquette from 1531, well before champagne sparkled on purpose. The tank method arrived around 1900, developed by Federico Martinotti and patented in apparatus form by Eugène Charmat (1907), and eventually made prosecco scalable into the world's best-selling sparkling wine — outselling champagne by volume since around 2013. Cava began in 1872 when Josep Raventós of Codorníu brought bottle fermentation to Penedès. Franciacorta formalized Italy's prestige traditional method in the 1960s–90s, England's chalk downs emerged as serious sparkling country in the 2000s, and pét-nat went from farmhouse relic to cult category in the 2010s.
production: Traditional method (cava, crémant, Franciacorta, English sparkling): a finished base wine referments in its bottle and ages on lees — cava requires 9 months (18 for Reserva, 30 for Gran Reserva), Franciacorta 18-plus — before riddling and disgorgement. Tank method (prosecco): secondary fermentation happens in large pressurized tanks over days to weeks, and the wine is filtered and bottled under pressure, cheaper and fruit-forward. Ancestral method (pét-nat): the wine goes into bottle while still fermenting, finishing there with no added sugar or yeast, usually undisgorged and lightly cloudy. The transfer method splits the difference, and simple carbonation — injected CO2 — marks the budget tier. Pressure ranges from gentle frizzante to full spumante.

styles.name: Prosecco
styles.description: Tank-method Glera from northeast Italy — pear, blossom, and easy charm; the world's volume leader.

styles.name: Cava
styles.description: Spain's traditional-method sparkler from Penedès — citrus and almond, remarkable value, with long-aged Gran Reservas.

styles.name: Crémant
styles.description: Traditional-method wines from several French regions — Alsace, Loire, Bourgogne, Limoux — champagne technique beyond Champagne.

styles.name: Franciacorta
styles.description: Lombardy's prestige DOCG — chardonnay and pinot noir aged 18 months or more on lees.

styles.name: Pét-nat
styles.description: Méthode ancestrale wines bottled mid-ferment — a single fermentation, crown caps, gentle fizz, and often a haze.

styles.name: English sparkling
styles.description: Traditional-method wines from Sussex and Kent chalk — the same seam as Champagne's — now winning blind tastings.

funFacts: Prosecco has outsold champagne by bottle count since about 2013.
Italy renamed the prosecco grape 'Glera' in 2009 so 'Prosecco' could be protected as a place name rather than a variety.
Limoux claims the world's first intentional sparkling wine — 1531, by Benedictine monks — a century before champagne's rise.

name: Fortified Wine
tagline: Wine plus spirit — port, sherry, madeira, and the vermouth revival.
overview: Fortified wines are strengthened with grape spirit to roughly 15–22% ABV, a practice born of the age of sail, when fortification kept wine sound on long voyages. Timing defines style: port (Douro Valley) adds spirit mid-fermentation, preserving sweetness; sherry (Jerez) fortifies after a fully dry ferment, then ages either biologically under a veil of flor yeast (fino, manzanilla) or oxidatively (oloroso), blended through fractional solera systems; madeira is deliberately heated and oxidized — estufagem tanks or attic canteiro casks — making it nearly indestructible. Sicily's marsala, France's vins doux naturels, and vermouth — wine aromatized with wormwood and botanicals, then fortified — complete the family. Long unfashionable, fortified wines are prized again by sommeliers and are the backbone of classic cocktails.
history: England's wars with France drove its merchants to Portugal — the 1703 Methuen Treaty favored Portuguese wine, and brandy stabilized Douro reds into port; the Douro was demarcated by royal decree in 1756, among the world's first appellations. Sherry, Shakespeare's 'sack,' had been England's darling since Falstaff. Madeira's odd genius emerged at sea: tropical crossings improved it (vinho da roda), and it became early America's cellar staple — famously poured to toast the Declaration of Independence in 1776. Antonio Benedetto Carpano bottled modern vermouth in Turin in 1786, feeding the cocktail age. Phylloxera, war, and fashion battered the category in the 20th century; the bar world led its rediscovery.
production: Port: Douro grapes (touriga nacional and kin) are trodden or robotically 'trodden' in lagares and fermented only to 5–7% before 77% grape spirit arrests the yeast, locking in sweetness around 19–20%; aging is oxidative in cask (tawny, colheita) or reductive with decades in bottle (vintage, declared only in top years). Sherry: palomino ferments bone dry, then is fortified to about 15% for flor-protected finos or 17%+ for oxidative olorosos, and matures through solera rows never fully emptied; sweet PX comes from sun-dried grapes. Madeira: wines are warmed for months in estufas or years under warm roofs, embracing oxidation. Vermouth: wine is infused with wormwood-led botanicals, sweetened, and fortified.

styles.name: Port
styles.description: The Douro's sweet fortified red — fruity rubies, nutty age-stated tawnies, and vintage ports that mature for half a century.

styles.name: Sherry
styles.description: Jerez's dry-to-sweet spectrum: saline fino and manzanilla under flor, nutty amontillado and oloroso, and syrupy pedro ximénez.

styles.name: Madeira
styles.description: Heat-aged island wine in four noble grades from dry sercial to rich malmsey — effectively immortal once opened.

styles.name: Marsala
styles.description: Sicily's fortified wine, from serious vergine soleras to the kitchen staple.

styles.name: Vermouth and aromatized wine
styles.description: Wormwood-bittered, botanical-infused fortified wines — Turin rosso, French dry — essential to the martini and negroni.

styles.name: Vins doux naturels
styles.description: France's muscats and grenaches (Beaumes-de-Venise, Banyuls, Maury) fortified mid-ferment in the port manner.

funFacts: An opened bottle of madeira can stay drinkable for months or years — heat and oxygen already did their worst during production.
The Douro's 1756 demarcation, marked with stone pillars, makes port one of the world's oldest legally defined wine regions.
Vermouth takes its name from Wermut, German for wormwood, its defining botanical.
